ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03301099); no specific packaged/flavoured RTD subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no dedicated flavoured packaged milk tea code exists. Closest parent category used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for packaged flavoured milk tea as of 2026.

allergens: milk (may contain dairy or non-dairy creamer derived from milk proteins)

serving.description: 1 standard packaged bottle or can (approximately 250 ml)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard packaged serving of flavoured milk tea is approximately 250 ml (250 g), equivalent to a typical single-serve bottle or can sold in Singapore convenience stores and supermarkets.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured Milk Tea (Packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged flavoured milk tea (cr_id D09020029). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this entry with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB entry; value estimated at 0.55 g/100g based on typical whole-milk and non-dairy creamer fat composition for RTD milk tea products (USDA FoodData Central reference: Milk, whole, 3.25% milkfat, NDB 01077, adapted proportionally to 0.93 g total fat). This estimation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for sweetened milk tea
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for packaged flavoured milk tea. GI of 55 is estimated based on the closest analogous entry: sweetened tea with milk (GI ~55), consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) which reports GI values for sweetened milk-based beverages in the 50–60 range. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 16.775) / 100 ≈ 9.2 per 250 g serving. GI of 55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney GI Database threshold (low ≤55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (6.26 g sugar/100 g) with added sugar exceeding the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold; independently associated with elevated blood pressure via insulin-mediated sympathetic activation and sodium retention pathways (AHA 2018).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage; the 6.26 g/100 g sugar load in a liquid matrix raises blood pressure risk independently of its low sodium content (22 mg/100 g), and the likely non-dairy creamer may contribute pro-inflammatory saturated fat — choose plain unsweetened brewed tea instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.26 g free sugar per 100 g and 0 g dietary fibre; classified as an SSB under Singapore HPB Rule 20, triggering the hard SSB gate for Type 2 Diabetes management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this packaged flavoured milk tea entirely; as a sugar-sweetened beverage with rapidly absorbed liquid sugar (GI ~55, GL ~9.2 per 250 ml serving) and no fibre buffer, it causes rapid postprandial glucose excursions — opt for plain brewed tea with no added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular risk through dyslipidaemia, visceral adiposity, and chronic low-grade inflammation; estimated saturated fat from non-dairy creamer adds further atherogenic potential.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this beverage; the 6.26 g/100 g added sugar contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial dysfunction with habitual intake, and the estimated 0.55 g/100 g saturated fat from likely palm-based non-dairy creamer may further elevate LDL cholesterol — replace with unsweetened plain tea.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (6.26 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; diluted tea extract in packaged RTD format does not provide sufficient polyphenol content to offset inflammatory burden.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this sweetened packaged milk tea; the added sugar load promotes inflammatory mediators and the likely palm-based non-dairy creamer contributes pro-inflammatory saturated fat — plain brewed green or black tea without sugar provides anti-inflammatory polyphenols without the sugar burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured Milk Tea (Packaged) is a ready-to-drink (RTD) commercially manufactured sweetened beverage widely sold in Singapore and across Southeast Asia in single-serve bottles, cans, or Tetra Pak cartons. It is produced by blending brewed black or green tea extract with milk or a non-dairy creamer, sugar, added flavourings (such as honey, taro, or original milk tea flavour), and stabilisers, then heat-treated (UHT or pasteurised) for shelf stability. The beverage has a smooth, mildly sweet, creamy-tea flavour with a light body and no significant bitterness. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 47 kcal, 0.79 g protein, 0.93 g fat, 6.71 g carbohydrate (of which 6.26 g is sugar), 0 g dietary fibre, and 22 mg sodium. The high free-sugar content (6.26 g/100 g) classifies this product as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore HPB guidelines, making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. A standard 250 ml serving delivers approximately 117.5 kcal and 15.65 g of rapidly absorbed sugar with no fibre buffer, resulting in a low glycemic index (~55) and a glycemic load of approximately 9.2 per serving. The product is ultra-processed and typically contains palm-based non-dairy creamers, which contribute saturated fat with potential pro-inflammatory and atherogenic effects. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast accompaniment or afternoon snack in Singapore, Malaysia, and broader Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id D09020029, confidence 0.95) which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central proportional adaptation and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for sweetened milk tea and documented. All mandatory multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine category.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.55 g/100 g) is estimated proportionally from USDA FoodData Central whole milk fat profile (NDB 01077) scaled to the reported total fat of 0.93 g/100 g, as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. This is an adapted estimate and is flagged accordingly. 2. The following fields are set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g. Reason: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this packaged RTD flavoured milk tea are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry D09020029, and no sufficiently close branded equivalent was found in USDA Branded Foods (USDA FoodData Central, searched April 2026) to supply these values. This is a confirmed data gap from the primary approved source; the -1 sentinel is used per Project Veritas v2 numeric hygiene rules to indicate unavailability rather than zero. 3. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; these are confirmed zero values per the HPB database entry D09020029. 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; consistent with a liquid beverage containing no whole-food fibre components. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database sweetened milk tea entries and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in approved sources. GI of 55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney threshold (low ≤55). 6. This product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore HPB Rule 20 and triggers SSB hard gates for Type 2 Diabetes and Hypertension health conditions. 7. Halal status is assumed based on typical commercial milk tea formulations in Singapore; consumers should verify individual product halal certification.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed from HPB source data; multilingual names added for Singaporean cuisine category; GI estimated from University of Sydney analogous entry.

Iteration 2: Documented all -1 sentinel fields in notes with confirmed data gap reason; corrected ontology match types to 'closest_parent'; adjusted gi_category to 'low' consistent with GI value of 55.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes section to explicitly list every -1 field with the specific reason (not reported in HPB Foodo D09020029 and not found in USDA Branded Foods as of April 2026) and the source consulted that confirmed unavailability, fully satisfying g3_numeric_hygiene requirements per the negative sentinel values rule.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
